

-- 1 of 21 --

MODULE #6:
Contractual Matters

-- 2 of 21 --

Key clauses:
 	What’s your name?
- Individual “doing business as … ”
- LLC
- Corporation
THIS MARKETING AGREEMENT (this “Agreement”) is made and
entered into as of January 1, 2014 (the “Effective Date”), by and
among XCOMPANY, a XSTATE corporation (the “Company”),
ResponseInk, LLC, a North Carolina limited liability company
(“Contractor”), and CLAYTON MAKEPEACE, an individual who
controls Contractor, and whose address is XADDRESS (the
“Principal”).

-- 3 of 21 --

Key clauses:
 	I’m free – are you?
- Represents
Contractor represents, warrants and covenants to the Company that:
(i) Contractor is not bound, nor will Contractor become bound, by any
covenant, contract, agreement or other obligation that conflicts with, or may or
does prevent Contractor in any manner from performing Contractor’s Services
to the Company under this Agreement, and
(ii) Contractor is not aware of any presently existing fact, circumstance or
event (including, without limitation, any legal constraint) which would preclude
or restrict it from providing to the Company the Services contemplated by this
Agreement, or which would give rise to any breach of any term or provision
hereof, or which could otherwise result in the termination of its engagement
hereunder for Cause or Good Reason (each as defined herein).

-- 4 of 21 --

Key clauses:
 	I’m free – are you?
- Representations
Principal’s Representations. Principal represents, warrants and covenants to
the Company that:
(i) Neither Principal nor any entities with which he is directly or indirectly
affiliated (collectively, “Affiliates”) is bound, nor will Principal or his Affiliates
become bound, by any covenant, contract, agreement or other obligation that
conflicts with, or may or does prevent Principal in any manner from performing
Services on behalf of the Contractor to the Company under this Agreement,
and
(ii) Principal is not aware of any presently existing fact, circumstance or event
(including, without limitation, any health condition or legal constraint) which
would preclude or restrict him from providing to the Company on behalf of the
Contractor the Services contemplated by this Agreement, or which would give
rise to any breach of any term or provision hereof, or which could otherwise
result in the termination of Contractor’s engagement hereunder for Cause or
Good Reason.

-- 5 of 21 --

Key clauses:
 	What are you looking for?
- Itemize components
- Schedule
Writer agrees to produce [job description] herein referred to as “The Work.”
Writer agrees to produce the Work as directed by LLC.
Work shall be delivered according to the following due schedule:
A first draft due (xxxx) (herein, "First Draft"), and the final revised copy due
(xxxx) (herein, "Final Copy").
Writer agrees to make revisions and/or, if necessary, a full re-write of the
Work so that it shall meet such standards, length and quality as required by
LLC.

-- 6 of 21 --

Key clauses:
 	What’s in it for me?
- Fee or advance
- Incentive
In exchange for Writer's performance as described in Section 1 above, LLC
shall compensate him according to the following schedule:
a) Twelve Thousand, Five Hundred Dollars ($12,500.00) upfront upon
execution of this Agreement.
b) Twelve Thousand, Five Hundred Dollars ($12,500.00) upon receipt and
approval of the Final Copy.
c) If LLC decides in its sole discretion, to use the Work following its initial test
mailing, Copywriter shall be compensated a royalty of five Cents ($0.05) for
every piece mailed to the public (herein "Royalty"). Royalty shall be paid
every time Work is used at this rate until LLC decides in its sole discretion to
cease use of the Work.

-- 7 of 21 --

Key clauses:
 	When do I get my incentive payments?
All royalties due Writer under this agreement will be paid in full on or before the
15th of the month after the Work was used.

-- 8 of 21 --

 	How long do I get paid?
 	How do I know you’re not cheating?
Writer shall receive royalty payments as defined herein each time the Word is
used to promote the LLC’s product.
LLC will provide Writer with a Mailing Report at the end of each month indicating
the number of pieces mailed in that month.
To confirm the accuracy of the Mailing Report, Writer shall have the right to
examine Post Office receipts or other documentation at his discretion.
Key clauses:

-- 9 of 21 --

Key clauses:
 	Pre-nup (kill fees)
If LLC decides in its sole discretion that the First Draft is inappropriate, this
Agreement shall terminate immediately and Writer may keep the initial payment
of Twelve Thousand, Five Hundred Dollars ($12,500.00) and forgo any and all
future payment.

-- 10 of 21 --

Key clauses:
 	Who owns the stuff?
Copyrights. The parties acknowledge that they do not anticipate that any
intellectual property will be created by Contractor or Principal pursuant to this
Agreement; however, Contractor and Principal also acknowledge that if any
intellectual property is created pursuant to this Agreement, all such materials,
intellectual property or other work (collectively, “Work”) produced, invented or
discovered by either of them at any time before or after the date of this
Agreement (and specifically including the period prior to the date of this
Agreement) as part of or in connection with the Services shall be deemed a
"work made for hire" for copyright purposes, and that all copyrights and any
other intellectual property rights of any kind in the Work shall be owned by the
Company.

-- 11 of 21 --

 	How can it be used?
No Restrictions on the Company’s Use of Work. So long as Contractor is
compensated as set forth herein, the Company shall not be restricted in any
way from modifying, reverse engineering or decompiling the Work or creating
derivative works based on the Work.
Key clauses:

-- 12 of 21 --

Key clauses:
 	What do we call this relationship?
- Independent contractor status
Independent Contractor Status. This Agreement is intended to create an
independent contractor relationship between Company and Contractor, and
shall be interpreted to effectuate such intent.
Contractor Determines Means and Methods. Except as expressly set forth in
this Agreement, Contractor shall be solely responsible for determining the
means and methods for performing the Services and Contractor shall
determine the time, place and manner in which it will provide the Services
within the overall schedule established by Company.

-- 13 of 21 --

Key clauses:
 	What do we call this relationship?
- Independent contractor status
Company has no Control Over Contractor. Company shall have no control or
supervision over Contractor’s employees, contractors, officers, directors,
representatives or affiliates, including, without limitation, the Principal.
Contractor shall not represent that Contractor or any of such parties is an
employee or affiliate of Company, and shall at all times represent Contractor
as an independent contractor of Company.
Neither Contractor nor the Principal shall under any circumstances be deemed
to be an employee or affiliate of Company for any purposes, including federal
tax purposes. This Agreement shall not be interpreted as a joint venture or
partnership between or among Contractor, Company and the Principal.

-- 14 of 21 --

Key clauses:
 	What do we call this relationship?
- Independent contractor status
Company will not Withhold Taxes. Company shall not withhold any taxes
from any payments made to Contractor pursuant to this Agreement.
Company has not, is not and shall not be obligated to make, and it is the sole
responsibility of Contractor to make, all periodic filings and payments
required to be made in connection with any withholding taxes, FICA taxes,
federal unemployment taxes, and any other federal, state or local taxes,
payments or filings required to be paid, made or maintained in connection
with any payments made to Contractor by Company.
Company has not, will not and shall not be obligated to provide workers’
compensation insurance for Contractor. Company has not, will not and shall
not be obligated to provide any minimum compensation, vacation pay, sick
leave or any other employee benefit to Contractor or any other party in
connection with this Agreement.

-- 15 of 21 --

Key clauses:
 	Confidentiality
Confidentiality. During the Engagement Period and at all times thereafter,
Contractor and Principal shall not in any manner, either directly or indirectly, (i)
use any part of the confidential information of the Company or of any of its
affiliates (the “Confidential Information described in a document to be provided
to Contractor and Principal”) except to perform the Services; or (ii) divulge,
disclose, distribute, reproduce, or communicate to any person or organization
any of the Confidential Information without the prior written consent of the
Company which may be withheld in the Company’s sole discretion.
In the event Contractor or Principal is requested pursuant to or required by
applicable law or regulation or by legal process to disclose any Confidential
Information, they agree to provide the Company with prompt notice of such
request or requirement to enable the Company to seek an appropriate
protective order, waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement or
take other appropriate action.

-- 16 of 21 --

Key clauses:
 	Confidentiality
Contractor and Principal agree to use their best efforts in such event to assist
the Company in obtaining a protective order. If, in the absence of a protective
order or the receipt of a waiver under this Agreement, Contractor or Principal is
nonetheless, in the written opinion of its or his counsel, compelled to disclose
the Confidential Information to any tribunal or else stand liable for contempt or
suffer other censure or significant penalty, Contractor and Principal, after
notice to the Company, may disclose to such tribunal only such Confidential
Information that they are is compelled to disclose.
Contractor and Principal shall not be liable for the disclosure of Confidential
Information to a tribunal compelling such disclosure unless such disclosure
was caused or resulted from a previous disclosure by Contractor and Principal
not permitted under this Agreement.

-- 17 of 21 --

 	Non-Compete
Contractor and Principal shall not, at any time during the Engagement Period,
directly or indirectly, individually or through its principal or as an owner, officer,
director, partner, employee, member, agent, consultant, representative,
independent contractor, or in any other capacity whatsoever of any
corporation or other entity, own, manage, operate, control, conduct or assist in
any way any Competing Business (including, without limitation, the
requirement that Contractor and Principal cannot perform services for any
other financial publisher, and can perform services only for persons and
entities not engaged in a Competing Business) throughout the world.
For purposes of this Agreement, “Competing Business” shall mean any
business that directly or indirectly is engaged in investment publishing
throughout the world.
Key clauses:

-- 18 of 21 --

Key clauses:
 	Responsibility for compliance
The Company assumes full responsibility to ensure that the Work is fully
compliant prior to its use.
The Work will be distributed to prospective buyers only after the Company’s
attorneys have found it to be in compliance with all governing laws and
regulations.
If any portion of the Work is found to be non-compliant, the Writer hereby agrees
to make the changes required to bring it into compliance.

-- 19 of 21 --

 	Indemnify and Hold Harmless
An “indemnification clause” obligates YOU to
compensate your client for losses or damages he
incurs as a result of using your copy.
A “hold harmless provision” effectively bars the
party responsible for indemnification from bringing
suit against the party being indemnified.
Key clauses:

-- 20 of 21 --

1. If you don’t understand something, ask.
2. Use surprise clauses as an excuse to negotiate.
3. Establish a relationship with a good lawyer.
4. Never sign anything you don’t understand.
5. Ask how compliance is handled.
6. If you’re not sure, check with other copywriters.
7. Get live signatures whenever possible.

-- 21 of 21 --
